one_line_takeaway: Charmer is an efficient query-based character-level attack that reaches ~100% attack success with tiny edit distances on BERT-class models and 7B LLMs, and empirically shows that popular typo-corrector defenses collapse (0.96% -> 98.09% ASR) once their artificial constraints are relaxed, pointing to adversarial training as the more promising defense.
key_finding: Charmer achieves >95% ASR on every studied benchmark and model while producing the smallest edit distances and among the highest similarities. On BERT/SST-2 it reaches 100% ASR (dlev 1.47, Sim 0.90) versus 95.16% for the strongest token baseline TextFooler, a 4.84-point ASR gain, and its USE similarity of 0.90 is 8 points above that same runner-up TextFooler (0.82). On Llama 2-Chat 7B/SST-2, Charmer-Fast hits 95.47% ASR vs TextFooler's 85.79%. Crucially for security: (1) as a defense inside adversarial training, Charmer cuts character-level ASR from 64.02% (standard) to 20.34% with only a modest clean-accuracy drop (92.43% -> 87.20%), whereas the token-level TextGrad defense actually worsens character-level robustness (ASR 67.34%, ~3.32 points above standard) and hurts clean accuracy (80.94%); (2) typo-corrector defenses are brittle: against the Jones et al. (2020) robust-encoding defense, ASR jumps from 0.96% to 98.09% simply by relaxing the lowercase-English-only constraint, and Charmer reaches 100% ASR on all studied typo-correctors when the artificial Pruthi-Jones Constraints are dropped.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Charmer, a query-based (black-box) character-level adversarial attack for NLP classifiers, and argues against two prevailing beliefs: that character-level attacks cannot exploit good position-selection heuristics and that they are easy to defend. Charmer's own novelty is a two-stage greedy search: (1) Algorithm 1, a top-n position pre-selection heuristic that scores each of the 2|S|+1 insertion/replacement positions by swapping in a single 'test' character (whitespace, U+0020) and measuring the change in loss, thereby removing dependence on sentence length; and (2) Algorithm 2, which at each of k iterations expands only those top-n positions over the full alphabet, evaluates the batch under the Carlini-Wagner loss, keeps the highest-loss sentence, and stops on misclassification. The perturbation space is formalized via an expansion/contraction operator pair (phi, psi) that lets a single 'replace' operation express insertions, deletions, and substitutions, giving a clean characterization of all sentences within Levenshtein distance k (Corollary 3.9). The authors also show Charmer works as a defense (inner maximizer for TRADES adversarial training) and use it to demonstrate that existing typo-corrector defenses give a false sense of robustness. Note: TextGrad, GBDA, HotFlip, DeepWordBug, Pruthi et al., and Jones et al. are prior work used as baselines/defenses, not contributions of this paper.
threat_model: Query-based / black-box evasion attack at inference time. The adversary has input-output query access to a text classifier (or an LLM used as a classifier via prompt wrapping SP1 + S + SP2) and seeks a minimally perturbed input S' that is misclassified while remaining semantically equivalent and imperceptible to humans. Imperceptibility is operationalized as a small Levenshtein (edit) distance between S and S', i.e., the adversarial set is S_k(S, Gamma) = sentences within edit distance k. Unlike token-level attacks that can alter sentence semantics, the character-level framing is claimed to keep meaning intact. The paper explicitly targets the practicality gap in prior defenses: robust word recognition / typo-corrector defenses (Pruthi et al. 2019; Jones et al. 2020) only hold under artificial 'Pruthi-Jones Constraints' (no repeat-word edit, no first/last char edit, no words under 4 chars, lowercase-English-only), which real attackers need not respect.
limitations: Scope is classification only (single-label and text-pair NLI/QNLI); the LLM evaluation likewise casts Llama 2 / Vicuna as classifiers via next-token probabilities rather than attacking open-ended generation or jailbreaking. Character-level 'semantics preservation' is asserted via Levenshtein distance and USE similarity but not validated with a human study in the paper's own experiments (the authors cite prior work, e.g., Dyrmishi et al., only to critique token attacks). Charmer is not the fastest method (DeepWordBug is faster), and the full version's runtime is comparable to TextGrad; the fast n=1 variant trades ASR and edit distance for speed. Results use TextAttack models and 7B open-weight LLMs; no closed/API models or larger LLMs are tested. Whether character-level or token-level robustness is the right target is left open, and defense results are limited to SST-2 with 1-epoch TRADES training.

quantitative_results: BERT/SST-2: Charmer 100.00% ASR, dlev 1.47, Sim 0.90 vs TextFooler 95.16% ASR (4.84-point ASR gain)
Adversarial training (BERT/SST-2): Charmer defense reduces char-level ASR 64.02% -> 20.34% (clean acc 92.43% -> 87.20%); TextGrad defense raises char-level ASR to 67.34% and drops clean acc to 80.94%
Typo-corrector bypass (Jones et al. 2020 robust encoding): ASR 0.96% -> 98.09% when relaxing the LowEng constraint; Charmer reaches 100% ASR on all typo-correctors without Pruthi-Jones Constraints
Position selection: n=20 chosen; SST-2 sentences have up to 489 and average 213.72 positions, so n=20 removes length dependence at ~0.25s extra cost over random selection
